name: Tom Jobim
description:
'''Antônio Carlos Brasileiro de Almeida Jobim''' (25 January 1927 – 8 December 1994), also known as '''Tom Jobim''' ({{IPA|pt|audio=Tom-Jobim-2.ogg|tõ ʒoˈbĩ}}), was a Brazilian composer, pianist, guitarist, songwriter, arranger and singer. Jobim is considered a great exponent of Brazilian music and one of the fathers of [[bossa nova]] for having merged [[samba]] with [[cool jazz]] in the 1960s as a pioneer of the genre. He is also regarded as one of the most celebrated songwriters of the 20th century, and his compositions have been played and recorded by many singers and instrumentalists internationally since the early 1960s.

Jobim is best known for his composition "[[The Girl from Ipanema|Garota de Ipanema (The Girl from Ipanema)]]", which has been recorded over 240 times by other artists, and is believed to be one of the most recorded popular songs in history. Along with "Garota de Ipanema," many of Jobim's compositions are included in [[Jazz standard|jazz]] and pop standard repertoires. Several of these compositions are featured on the 1964 album ''[[Getz/Gilberto]]'', which won the [[Grammy Awards]] for [[Grammy Award for Best Jazz Instrumental Album, Individual or Group|Best Jazz Instrumental Album – Individual or Group]], [[Grammy Award for Best Engineered Album, Non-Classical|Best Engineered Album, Non-Classical]], and was the first jazz record to win [[Grammy Award for Album of the Year|Album of the Year]]. The recording of "Garota de Ipanema" that features on this album also won [[Grammy Award for Record of the Year|Record of the Year]] when released as a single. His 1967 album with [[Frank Sinatra]], ''[[Francis Albert Sinatra & Antônio Carlos Jobim]]'', was nominated for Album of the Year in 1968 and album ''[[Antônio Brasileiro]]'' was awarded the 1995 [[Grammy Award for Best Latin Jazz Album]].
